title: Regulation of Charlatans in High-Skill Professions
abstract: We model a market for a skill in short supply and high demand, where the presence of charlatans (professionals who sell a service they do not deliver on) is an equilibrium outcome. In the model, reducing the number of charlatans through regulation lowers consumer surplus because of the resulting reduction in competition among producers. Producers can benefit from this reduction, potentially explaining the regulation we observe. The effect on total surplus depends on the type of regulation. We derive the factors that drive the cross-sectional variation in charlatans (regulation) across professions.

article.introduction:
“We write today to ask what specific steps FINRA will take to address the pervasive misconduct among its financial advisers and to protect families looking to invest their hard-earned savings.” U.S. Senators E. Warren and T. Cotton, Letter to FINRA May 11, 2016
An important role for governmentin the modern economy concerns the regulation of standards and information disclosure for goods and services. As the introductory quote from Senators Warren and Cotton illustrates, the justification that legislators use for this role is generally the protection of consumers. Indeed, the assumption that regulation is in consumers' interest is often taken for granted. In this paper, we argue that this assumption requires more rigorous justification. We demonstrate in our model that in highly regulated sectors of the economy for goods and services that are in short supply and are highly valued by most consumers, setting standards reduces consumer surplus.
We study the role of regulation in a parsimonious equilibrium model that features charlatans—agents who know that they do not possess the skill to provide a particular service but nevertheless enter that profession and offer the service. In the model, skilled agents are in inelastic short supply and consumers receive information about the professional's skill by observing a signal. To understand why regulation reduces consumer surplus, note that before any government intervention, the probability that the consumer could be facing a charlatan is reflected in a lower price for the service. If the government imposes a standard by requiring a lower bound on providers' signals, the price of the service increases to reflect the lower probability of consumers facing a charlatan. If this was the only effect, consumers would be left indifferent: the increase in their expected benefit is offset by the higher price. However, there is an additional effect: the standard reduces the number of providers, thereby reducing competition. This reduction in competition increases prices further and thus decreases consumer surplus.
One may wonder why, in the absence of a government-imposed standard, the market is not flooded with charlatans. The reason is that even without government regulation a market standard exists—consumers only hire producers who, in expectation, satisfy the market-clearing quality threshold. So the question regulators face is not whether there should be standards, but rather whether it is in consumers' interests to impose a higher standard than the one already imposed by the market. Under our assumptions, the answer to this question is no.
Perhaps most surprisingly, our results extend to information disclosure requirements. It is often argued that better informing consumers about the products they buy allows them to make better decisions thereby increasing consumer welfare.1Although this logic does imply that overall welfare increases with disclosure, it does not imply that consumer welfare increases because it ignores the equilibrium effect of information revelation on prices. Consider a case where the government is perfectly informed about who the charlatans are and, instead of setting standards, communicates this information to consumers, who are homogeneous. Even though this information drives charlatans out of the market, consumers are left worse off because as a group the total benefit they derive from the service is no larger (i.e., the supply of skill has not been reduced) but the reduction in competition forces them to pay more for the service. In fact, when consumers are homogeneous the additional price increase that follows the disclosure removes all consumer surplus. When consumers are heterogeneous, so long as enough consumers value the service highly and the service is in short enough supply, consumer surplus is also reduced with disclosure.
Although overall welfare within a sector (i.e., the total welfare of all producers and consumers within the sector) is rarely cited by policy makers as the reason to regulate, one might argue that maximizing overall welfare within the sector should be the policy objective. However, even under this objective, it is difficult to justify regulation in our setting. By allowing for better matching of producers and consumers, information disclosure increases overall welfare. At the same time, disclosure increases producer welfare through higher wages. Because producer interests are aligned with overall welfare, one would not expect there to be much need to require information disclosure—producers should credibly disclose themselves. Standards, however, have the opposite effect: introducing a government-imposed minimum standard reduces overall welfare by reducing the supply of skill. Thus imposing standards is also undesirable from an overall welfare perspective. In both cases, if the objective is to maximize overall welfare within the sector, there appears to be little need to regulate in our model.
In our model, the group that benefits from regulation is skilled producers. Although charged with regulating the finance and medical professions, FINRA and the American Medical Association (AMA) are actually trade groups that represent producer interests. A natural question that arises is what standard would these trade groups favor? Producers trade off the benefit of reducing the number of charlatans (type 2 error) against the cost of a standard that catches too many unlucky skilled workers (type 1 error). The entry decision of charlatans (as well as the resulting equilibrium wage in the profession), depends on (i) the opportunity cost required to enter the profession, (ii) the amount of uncertainty surrounding the professional's ability, and (iii) the degree to which the skill is in short supply. When trade groups like FINRA set standards, professions with skills in relatively larger supply, shorter training periods, and less informative signals regarding the professional's skill are more likely to feature charlatans.
We begin our analysis in the SectionII, where we specify the model. We then apply the model, in the SectionIII, to the case in which consumers are homogeneous. SectionIVextends the analysis to include heterogeneous consumers who value the good or service differently. In SectionV, we investigate the implications of allowing skilled producers to set standards. We then discuss the generality of our results in SectionVI. The final section concludes the paper. In the next section, we explain how this paper fits into the existing literature.
article.I. Background:
We focus on goods or services for which the customer cannot verify that the provider is actually providing the good or service—what is known in the literature as “credence goods.”2Because of this asymmetry, governments often regulate such goods through certification and licensing requirements.3The difference between these two types of regulation is that a professional cannot practice without a license but can practice without being certified. Consequently, we can interpret a licensing requirement as a minimum standard and certification as information disclosure. The existing literature comes down in favor of certification because it provides consumers with information they otherwise would not have without reducing competition by inadvertently denying licenses to skilled professionals. However, this is not the end of the story. In this paper, we show that under our assumptions, certification still reduces competition by revealing which producers are charlatans, leaving consumers worse off. In contrast, if the regulators objective is to help skilled professionals by increasing their wages, then licensing is preferred over certification because it both discloses information and further decreases competition by not allowing some skilled workers to practice. In summary, in our setting, certification is never preferred, not by producers nor by consumers.
Our paper is related to several studies on the importance of occupational licensing. The first paper to argue that licensing could hurt consumers through higher prices, lower quantity, and lower quality, is an empirical study of wages in independent professional practices by Friedman and Kuznets (1945).4Another related contribution is Arrow (1963), who discusses several aspects of the health care industry, including product uncertainty, supply conditions, and the organization of insurance. He notes that licensing requirements are usually defended to guarantee a minimum level of quality, but he does not offer a formal model of licensing requirements or certification. Our model sheds light on the conditions under which a minimum level of quality can be achieved but it also shows that under quite general conditions the imposition of a quality threshold makes consumers worse off. Stigler (1971) argues that licensing is an effective barrier to entry because occupational practice without the license is a criminal offense. He also argues that since much occupational licensing is performed at the state level, licensing can be used to study the characteristics of an occupation that give it political power. He considers several different explanatory variables for licensing including the size of the occupation, whether there is a national market for the profession, and the income per capita of the profession. The paper does not provide a formal model of licensing and certification with charlatans.
Shapiro (1986) provides a formal model of occupational licensing that requires minimum levels of human capital investment by professionals. Consequently, he does not focus on professions that require skills in short supply but instead he focuses on the employee's investment decisions when there is a perfectly elastic supply of identical potential producers. By raising training levels, licensing helps alleviate the moral hazard problem associated with underinvestment in human capital. He shows that licensing and certification tend to benefit consumers who value quality highly at the expense of those who do not, and that licensing may raise total surplus if sellers' investments are not observable, but is Pareto-reducing if training levels are observable. In contrast, Leland (1979) ignores moral hazard and instead like us focuses on the effect of asymmetric information. His model features a continuum of goods of different quality about which the government is fully informed but consumers are uninformed. Like Shapiro (1986), the good is in perfectly elastic supply, implying that when standards are introduced, the resulting price increase spurs the entry of higher quality goods, an effect that does not occur in our model. In our model, price increases spur the entry of charlatans, but that does not raise quality. This key difference explains Leland's main result, that standards can sometimes benefit consumers. Another difference between the two papers is that we study a homogeneous good that is bought by heterogeneous consumers, whereas Leland (1979) studies heterogenous goods that are bought by homogeneous (uninformed) consumers. As a consequence, Leland (1979) studies how the supply of actual product quality changes with regulation, whereas we study how the entry of charlatans is affected by regulation.
Our paper is also related to the industrial organization literature that studies matching. Like us, that literature studies equilibria in which agents are matched with each other, typically, in a principal-agent relationship.5Consequently, a primary focus of that literature is on optimal contracting issues, rather than regulation of the market. Translated to our setting, consumers are the principals and producers are the agents, so our paper can be thought of as investigating the conditions under which regulation benefits principals. A paper in this vain is Inderst and Ottaviani (2012c), who study optimal contracting in the market for financial advice.6Like us they consider the effect of information revelation, in this case, the specifics of the contract. They show that when consumers are naive and do not take into account the conflict of interest between broker compensation and their investment advice, mandating public disclosure of contracts can make consumers better off. However, using a two-sided matching model similar to ours, Chang and Szydlowski (2020) show that when consumers do take this conflict of interest into account, regulating contracts (to mitigate the conflict of interest) reduces information quality but does not change customer welfare. The reason is that regulation reduces fees for all providers, leading to less information provision, leaving consumers indifferent in equilibrium. Another related paper is Fuchs and Garicano (2010) who examine whether the matching between experts and problems should be accomplished by markets or firms. While firms are better at assessing an expert's fit, matching through markets ensures higher levels of utilization of experts. As in our model, their model features the possibility that unskilled agents pose as skilled, as well as the possibility that unskilled agents flood the market, leading to a form of market breakdown. Introducing regulation that keeps unskilled agents out can help avoid this type of market breakdown and improves total surplus (the sum of producer and consumer surplus), just as in our model. Lester et al. (2019) study these effects in a frictional market featuring imperfect competition with adverse selection. They find that in this context, it is possible for overall welfare to decrease when informational asymmetries are reduced or competition is increased.
We are not the first paper to point out that revealing information can leave consumers worse off. Dranove and Satterthwaite (1992) show that when consumers face search costs, although revealing information about product price and quality can reduce these costs, producers respond by reducing product quality, ultimately leaving consumers worse off. In Schlee (1996), a monopolist producer changes its level of production in response to the information revelation and thus, under special conditions such as an increasing returns-to-scale production function or highly convex marginal costs, consumers can be left worse off. Boone and Pottersz (2006) consider an economy in which the disclosure of information increases demand and thus drives prices up, leading to lower consumer surplus. Bergemann and Pesendorfer (2007) study an auction in which a monopolist producer chooses the optimal amount of information to reveal to maximize her own surplus, which necessarily implies that consumers will be left worse off. Ottaviani and Moscarini (1999) study a duopoly in which a single buyer has better information about product quality than the two sellers and derive conditions under which biased buyers are better off with lower quality information. Cosandier, Garcia, and Knauff (2018) show that in the case of a duopoly, when prices are strategic substitutes, increased price transparency can leave consumers worse off.
An insight from our analysis is that skilled producers benefit from information disclosure. Consequently, as Grossman (1981) points out, because skilled producers will voluntarily disclose, there is no reason to require disclosure. What is not obvious from Grossman (1981) is that even though disclosure increases overall surplus, it reduces consumer surplus. In the context of a natural experiment, Buonanno and Pagliero (2020) investigate the cross-sectional variation in standards that results when skilled producers choose the standard. They focus on competition between legislative districts and show that districts with higher wages set higher standards.
The key driver of our results is the endogeneous level of monopolistic competition in a model that does not feature frictions such as search costs or consumer naivete. This endogeneity distinguishes our model from models such as Milgrom and Weber (1982) that consider the effect of information on competition. Those models feature one monopolistic producer and hence the effect on competition comes entirely through consumers—information causes consumers to compete more fiercely with each other. Ganuza (2004) also considers a single-seller monopolistic setting in which an auctioneer wishes to sell an object to one ofrisk-neutral bidders with heterogeneous preferences. The auctioneer does not know bidders' preferences but has private information about the object's characteristics and must decide how much information to reveal prior to the auction. He finds that the auctioneer has an incentive to withhold information from the bidders, which is opposite of what we find. The reason for this difference is that in the setting of Ganuza (2004), the heterogeneous buyers become more homogeneous when they have less information and therefore compete more strongly with each other. In our setting, the release of information about producers reduces competition between producers, thereby hurting consumers.
article.II. The Model:
Our objective in this paper is to study a labor market that features professionals who have a skill that is in short supply and large demand. The resulting wages are high, so the profession is very attractive to producers. Thus, all agents who have this skill choose to produce, and agents who do not have the skill may also choose to offer the service.
Consider a unit measure of unskilled agents,, who consume a product or service that requires a skill in short supply. In addition, a measureof agents exist who have a skill that allows them to produce this product or service. The unskilled agents can pose as skilled agents, what we term “charlatans.” A charlatan cannot produce the product or service and thus confers no benefit. If an agent chooses to provide the product requiring the skill (whether skilled or not), he earns a wage paid by consumers that is determined in equilibrium. If the agent chooses not to provide the service, she earns a reservation wage that we normalize to zero. All agents know whether they are skilled or not.
The unskilled agents in our model are also consumers, so the measure of potential consumers is one. If an agent chooses to consume, then she must find a single producer who will provide the product or service. A producer can only service one consumer,7and thusis the maximum fraction of consumers who can consume the skill in short supply. That there are more potential producers than potential consumers captures the idea that charlatans have the potential to “flood” the market. If enough of them enter, a high-quality seller's market becomes a low-quality buyer's market.
At the other extreme, when consumers have no information on the producers' skill level, the resulting equilibrium features a wage of zero. The reason is that for any positive wage, all agents choose to produce (offer the service), implying that the supply of the skill is, which is strictly larger than the demand of one, implying that wages cannot be positive. With zero wages, both skilled workers and charlatans are indifferent about producing, so a continuum of equilibria exists. In equilibria that feature skilled producers, consumers pay nothing but get something, implying that consumer surplus is positive.
To allow for a market that features charlatans and positive wages in equilibrium, we provide consumers with information. Before we introduce a general information structure, it is useful to first illustrate the resulting equilibrium in the context of a simple example.
The timing is as follows. Agents first decide if they are going to generate a signal by comparing the expected wage of producing to their reservation wage. Because their reservation wage is zero, they will generate a signal so long as expected wages are positive. One can think of the signal as the product of an apprenticeship program (like a medical residency), or a grade on a required examination, and could even include soft information like word-of-mouth recommendations. Given a signal, agents then decide if they still want to produce. Because attending university or undertaking an apprenticeship program might require permanently giving up other opportunities, we allow for the possibility that agents face an opportunity cost. Specifically, if after generating the signal the agent chooses not to produce, his reservation wage drops by. Agents therefore choose to produce so long as their wage, given their signal realization, exceeds their reservation wage,.10
Letbe the solution to (9), that is, the signalthat consumerchooses to consume. Then define the inverse function, that is, the consumerwho chooses to consume a signal.
It is impossible for an equilibrium to exist that does not feature charlatan entry. To see why, suppose such an equilibrium does exist. In this equilibrium, all consumers believe that all producers are skilled, which implies that no producer incurs the opportunity cost. Because, all producers are hired for a positive wage, implying that all producers have positive expected wages. Charlatans will therefore choose to enter the market, which is a contradiction.
We restrict attention to equilibria in pure strategies, that is, we focus on equilibria in which every charlatan is strictly better off obtaining a signal so. Analyzing mixed strategy and sunspot equilibria in which charlatans are indifferent between entering significantly complicates the analysis without adding economic insight.11PropositionA.1derives the conditions under which a pure-strategy equilibrium exists and characterizes that equilibrium. When there is no opportunity cost (), then the only equilibrium of the model is a pure-strategy equilibrium which always exists. Pure-strategy equilibria also exist so long as the opportunity costis not too large.
To investigate the effect of licensing, we next derive the equilibrium in the economy with the requirement that a producer may only produce if her signal exceeds an exogenously determined lower bound. Obviously, in any equilibrium without a minimum standard, introducing a minimum standard belowhas no effect. So we only consider the case in which the exogenously imposed minimum standard exceeds the market imposed standard, that is,.
In AppendixB, we derive the conditions for existence of an equilibrium with a minimum standard. PropositionB.1shows that for any minimum standard that exceedsan equilibrium always exists. The intuition is similar to the derivation of the equilibrium without a standard but now the minimum standard is exogenously set rather than determined in equilibrium. Because the standard is higher than, not all consumers can consume. The constant of integration in the wage function is therefore set such that the marginal consumer is indifferent between consuming or not, rather than before where it was set to ensure that the marginal producer was indifferent between producing or not. Thus, a lower quality buyer's market becomes a higher quality seller's market.
There are two crucial differences between this equilibrium and the equilibrium without a minimum standard. First, a no-charlatan equilibrium exists—so long as the opportunity cost is positive, it is possible to set the standard high enough that the equilibrium will feature no charlatan entry. Second, when a standard is introduced, a buyer's market becomes a seller's market and so wages strictly increase. The first reason wages increase is that they no longer need to perform the role of keeping charlatans out—the minimum signal has that role. What this implies is that when, wages are equal to the expected benefit minus a term that takes into account the degree of competition from other skilled producers currently in the market but not potential producers (currently not in the market), as in the case without a minimum standard. The second reason wages increase is that the standard also reduces competition within the market because. Notice that when the minimum standard is set so high that no unskilled worker chooses to be a charlatan, all producers earn the same wage (because they are identical). Competition between them reduces the wage to the benefit conferred by the producer with the lowest allowable signal.
We are now ready to investigate how regulation affects consumer surplus. We begin by considering the case in which all consumers are homogeneous.
article.III. Homogeneous Consumers:
In summary, when consumers are homogeneous, revealing who the charlatans are always makes them worse off. But an even stronger result holds. As the following proposition (proved in AppendixC) shows, consumers are worse off with the revelation ofanyinformation.
Proposition 1.In any equilibrium with homogeneous consumers, consumer surplus is strictly increasing in.

Proposition 2.In any equilibrium with homogeneous consumers, overall welfare in the sector is strictly decreasing in.
Because the sum of consumer and producer surplus is decreasing inand consumer surplus is increasing, producer surplus must be decreasing in. Similarly, because skilled producers' share of producer surplus is decreasing in, skilled producer surplus is also decreasing in.
Proposition 3.Introducing an exogenous minimum standard into an economy with homogeneous consumers reduces consumer surplus.
The reason for this stark result is that by introducing a standard, a buyer's market is turned into a seller's market, that is, consumer demand outstrips supply. That means some consumers must choose not to consume. Because all consumers are identical, they all must therefore be indifferent between consuming and not consuming, and hence they compete away all of the surplus.
article.IV. Heterogeneous Consumers:
Heterogeneity introduces two important effects. The first is the price response to charlatan entry. When all consumers are identical, they value the service equally, so the price only responds to the change in supply and the likelihood of encountering a charlatan. The price does not drop because the marginal consumer values it less. When consumers are heterogenous, the price drop also reflects the fact that because there are more producers, the marginal consumer values the service less. This effect increases consumer surplus. The second effect is that more charlatans imply worse matches, that is, by servicing more consumers, the likelihood that a low-value consumer actually consumes goes up. This effect reduces consumer surplus.12
Which of the these two effects dominates depends on the supply of skill () and the distribution of tastes. To see why, note that even when (22) is satisfied, if the skill is in large enough supply so that all consumers can consume it (i.e.,), then in the full information equilibrium the wage is zero and all the surplus goes to consumers. Introducing uncertainty into this equilibrium reduces the likelihood of consumers consuming the skill (because some skilled producers will not pass the market standard), which reduces consumer surplus. On the other hand, whenis small, wages are high in the full information equilibrium, implying that most of the surplus goes to producers. Introducing uncertainty drops wages significantly. This drop more than overcomes the lower likelihood of actually consuming the skill, leading to higher consumer surplus. The effect on consumer surplus therefore depends on. The following proposition (proved in AppendixE) states that if a market exists in which the skill is in short enough supply, disclosure of information reduces (17), total consumer surplus:
Proposition 4.When (22) is satisfied there exists asuch that in any equilibrium of any economy in which, total consumer surplus is strictly increasing in.

One may be concerned that the upper bound derived in this proposition might be very small and hence the result might only apply when the supply of skilled producers is very small. To show that this is not the case, in Figure1we explicitly calculate the upper bound as a function offor different distributions of consumer valuations. The solid black curve assumes that consumer valuations are distributed Uniform[0,1], the dashed black curve assumes that consumer valuations are distributedon the unit inverval [0,1], and the colored curves assume Normally distributed valuations with unit standard deviation and means of(blue), 1 (red), 2 (orange), and 3 (green). Under the assumption that consumers have free disposal, negative valuations make no sense, so we truncate the Normal distributions at zero (see AppendixGfor more details). The main takeaway from the figure is that although ability to acquire the skill must be limited to a fraction of the population, it does not have to be implausibly rare. Even when the signal noise is very high (so the signals are close to uninformative) and valuations are distributed Normally with mean(so a significant fraction of consumers do not place high value on the service),. When the fraction of consumers who do not place a high value on the service is low, for example, when the mean of the Normal distribution is 3, then when the signal is close to uninformative,is 0.7. As the signal noise decreases,increases significantly, so that for informative signals (), when valuations are Normal with mean,. When the signal is close to perfectly informative, the bound approaches one, the point at which the skill is no longer in short supply (all consumers can consume).
Upper bound onfor different willingness-to-pay distributions. The figure plots, the upper bound in Proposition4(the highestfor which consumer surplus is increasing in the signal noise) as a function of the signal noise,. Each plot represents a different distribution of consumer willingness to pay. The black curve assumes that consumer valuations are distributed Uniform[0,1] and the colored curves assume Normally distributed valuations with unit standard deviation and means of(blue), 1 (red), 2 (orange), and 3 (green). Because we assume that consumers have free disposal, we truncate the Normal distributions at zero. The dashed black curve assumes that consumer valuations are distributedon the unit interval [0,1]. The bound is calculated by finding the root of (E.5).
As in the case with homogeneous consumers, overall welfare is increased with the disclosure of information. To understand why, recall that consumer heterogeneity introduces two additional effects of information disclosure, namely, the price response and better matching. However, overall welfare is affected only by the second effect. As we pointed out above, better information allows consumers who value the service most to consume it, implying that producers and consumers are better matched. Smallertherefore leads to an increase in overall welfare. But since overall welfare is already decreasing inwith homogeneous consumers, it must also be decreasing inin the heterogenous case. Using the same logic as before, this also implies that both producer surplus and skilled producer surplus are increasing in.
The fact that, by imposing a minimum standard, it is possible to have an equilibrium that contains no charlatans raises the question of whether such an equilibrium is desirable. The next proposition states (and proved in AppendixF) that imposing a standard always hurts consumers:
Proposition 5.Introducing an exogenous minimum standard into an economy with heterogeneous consumers reduces total consumer surplus.

Notice that Proposition5does not require that the service be highly valued by most consumers or that it be in short supply—there is no restriction on the shape ofor. The reason is that the two additional effects discussed above now both work in the same direction. Introducing a standard above the market standard leads less consumers to consume and simultaneously raises the price because the standard raises the willingness to pay of the marginal consumer.
One might wonder how any government imposed minimum standard in a market for professional services in a field like medicine (which has high costs when disease is left untreated) always results in a loss of consumer surplus. Surely, a minimum standard that prevents the worst diseases from being untreated would be beneficial to consumers? But the point to realize is that labor markets without government imposed minimum standards havemarketimposed standards. Wages adjust so that no producer with a signal below the market standard chooses to produce. For a profession like medicine, most consumers highly value successful treatment. Without a government imposed minimum standard, receiving quality treatment has a high price because many potential producers fail to make the market standard, which reduces supply. The insight is that with a government imposed minimum standard, that price is even higher because there is even less supply. By allowing the market, rather than the government, to sort out skilled producers from charlatans, consumers benefit.
For the same reasons as before, total welfare in the sector is reduced when a standard is introduced. Similarly, the benefit for producers depends on the standard that is introduced. If one takes the perspective that the licensing standards we see reflects the interests of trade groups representing skilled producers, then the wide variation in standards across professions will reflect cross-sectional differences in the trade-offs that trade groups in these professions face. With that in mind, in the next section we investigate how cross-sectional differences in professions affect the licensing standards that trade groups favor.
article.V. Understanding the Variation in Licens:
To investigate the implications of our model for the cross-sectional distribution of standards, we solve for the standard that maximizes the expected wages of skilled workers as a function of the fraction of skilled agents,, the informativeness of the signal,, and the opportunity cost,. To undertake this exercise, we make the simplifying assumption that consumer valuations are Uniformly distributed, so.
When a trade group sets standards, it does so by trading off the benefit of reducing the number of charlatans against the cost that some unlucky skilled workers will be denied the opportunity to work. So the question here is, when is it optimal for the trade group to set a standard so high that it discourages all charlatan entry? Notice that when, no standard will accomplish this goal. Because unskilled workers face no cost to become charlatans, it is impossible to discourage them. At the other extreme, whenis very large andis low, it will be possible to pick a standard that is low enough that most skilled workers are able to meet the standard but most unskilled workers will not. Given the high opportunity cost, the unskilled workers will find it suboptimal to become charlatans and the equilibrium will feature only skilled workers. Consequently, when the trade group is allowed to pick a standard that maximizes the expected wages of skilled workers, there will exist a set of parameters under which equilibria will feature charlatans and a complimentary set where equilibria will not feature charlatans.
Figure2plots these two sets for different values of(the supply of skilled producers). Points to the left of the curve are values ofandthat result in equilibria that feature charlatans. Points to the right are equilibria that do not feature charlatans. As we pointed out above, whenthere is no cost to becoming a charlatan, so all equilibria feature charlatans. Counterintuitively, whenis small, it does not take much of an increase infor the trade group to choose a standard high enough to discourage charlatan entry. When skill is in short supply one would expect very high wages, and so at first glance one might suppose that it would be difficult to keep charlatans out. But, in fact, the equilibrium effect goes the other way. Because the skill is in such short supply, without a minimum standard the equilibrium is dominated by charlatans and thus features very low wages. Setting a standard high enough to keep charlatans out is not that costly in this case because the skilled workers who are denied work would have earned low wages anyway in the equilibrium with no minimum standard. So, from the trade group's perspective, the optimal policy is to set a very high standard (see Figure3, Panel (a), top left), which implies that even for a small opportunity cost, the no-charlatan equilibrium (albeit with very small supply of skilled workers) prevails. Conversely, whenis high, wages in the equilibrium without standards are high and thus it is costly for the trade group to set a high standard (see Figure3, Panel (a), bottom right).
Charlatans in equilibrium. The blue, red, yellow, and purple lines represent equilibria with, and 0.3, respectively. Each curve plots the boundary between equilibria that feature charlatans and equilibria that do not, under the assumption that the trade group picks the optimal minimum standard as a function of the informativeness of the signal () and the opportunity cost (). Points to the left of the curve are values ofandthat result in equilibria that feature charlatans. Points to the right are equilibria that do not feature charlatans.
Minimum standards and consumer surplus when the trade group sets standards. Each panel is (a) the minimum standard imposed by the trade group or (b) the resulting consumer surplus in an equilibrium in which the trade group sets the standard, as a function of the informativeness of the signal () and the opportunity cost (). The individual plots vary the supply of skill in the economy, that is, starting with the top left plot in each panel and moving clockwise, and 0.2. The “cliff” in the plots corresponds to the demarcation (plotted in Figure2) between equilibria that feature charlatans and ones that do not.
The trade-off skilled producers face is consistent with the observed variation between regulation in medicine and finance. Doctors face very high opportunity costs allowing trade groups to impose very high standards, effectively keeping all charlatans out of the market. That is consistent with the very high pass rates on the medical boards and the lack of any meaningful sanctions for medical malpractice.13Finance, in contrast, has low opportunity costs, implying that the market likely features charlatan entry. Consistent with this view, the licensing exams feature lower passing rates and there are very large sanctions for misconduct.14
When the trade group chooses to set a high standard and the result is an equilibrium that does not feature charlatans, the lack of competition translates into very low consumer surplus (see Figure3, Panel (b), top left). Competition is reduced for two reasons. First, there are no charlatans. Second, because high standards are required, the standard reduces the supply of skilled agents, further reducing competition. This is particularly true when the signal is uninformative because very high standards are needed to keep charlatans out (see Figure3, Panel (a), top left). When the signal is informative, standards can be set to discourage charlatans without significantly reducing the supply of skilled workers. Thus, for low, consumer surplus is higher. So one way to stop the trade group from setting high standards is to have an informative signal. Thus, as Figure3demonstrates, revealing information can benefit consumers in a second-best world where standards are set by trade groups. Of course, trade groups themselves have the same incentive to reveal information, so it is not clear how a government mandate could improve information revelation.
article.VI. Discussion:
The explanation we explore in this paper for the explosive growth in the last 50 years of licensing requirements15is that trade groups are successful in lobbying governments to act in their interests. If this is not the explanation, then one of the explicit or implicit assumptions of our model that leads to this conclusion has to be violated. In this section, we identify the assumptions that we believe are most critical for our results.
Our model provides benchmark results for a labor market where skill is in short supply. Because all of the people who have the skill are already supplying it, there is no supply response to an increase in wages. The extent to which our model applies to professions in which the skill is in larger supply depends on the elasticity of the supply of skilled professionals to wages. In labor markets that require an extensive training period, this elasticity is likely to be low in the short term. For such labor markets, our model applies in the short term.
In the long term, the spurring of skilled entry into such professions could make consumers better off. The existing literature, beginning with Leland (1979), has focused on the effect of entry. The theoretical conditions under which regulation spurs entry and leaves consumers better off are therefore well understood.
If indeed the supply response explains the increased trend in regulation, we should see cross-sectional variation across professions in the amount of regulation: professions, like finance and medicine with skills in short supply should have little regulation and while professions with skills in higher supply like real estate agents should be more heavily regulated. Anecdotally, at least, the evidence appears to go the other way—medical doctors are clearly more regulated than real estate agents. Future empirical work could focus on clarifying these relationships.
Another assumption in our model is that all participants are fully rational. In particular, consumers appropriately take into account the probability of dealing with a charlatan. If, instead, prices of services do not accurately reflect that probability, for example, because consumers are naive, government intervention to protect consumers from themselves could potentially be beneficial.16Effective government policy in such cases requires that the government be more rational than consumers and well informed about consumer preferences and beliefs. It also requires that the benefit of mitigating the irrationality is large enough to overcome the adverse effect of regulation on competition.
In this article we choose to focus on the narrow question of whether regulation on disclosure and standards helps consumers largely because policy makers use consumer interests as justification for the regulation we see. We also address the question of how regulation affects overall welfare within the industry. However our paper does not address the wider question of how regulation affects the overall welfare of all participants across all industries in the economy. It is possible that regulation on disclosure and standards in all industries is welfare enhancing for the economy as a whole. We leave this question to future theoretical and empirical research.
Thus far we have assumed that when a consumer purchases the service from a charlatan, he or she gets nothing. But in some cases the possibility exists that the charlatans may actually do harm. One might suppose that our results are sensitive to this assumption, that is, in professions in which charlatans can harm consumers it might be in consumers' interests to regulate. In fact, as we show in the addendum paper (Berk and van Binsbergen,2019), even when charlatans can harm consumers, it is never in consumers' interests to impose a standard. In short, the possibility that charlatans might harm consumers does not justify a licensing requirement.
Where the presence of harm can impact our results is the effect of information revelation (certification). We show in the addendum paper that consumers are only better off with information revelation when the harm is high, and even then, only if two further conditions hold: the opportunity cost and the informativeness of the signal must both be low. To understand why, take the extreme case in which a charlatan inflicts extensive harm but bears no opportunity cost. In this case, without information revelation, the market almost breaks down. Only those consumers who value the service most would take the risk of being serviced by a charlatan, and even then they only pick producers with very high signals—that is, the market-imposed standard is very high, so very few consumers are serviced, leading to low overall surplus. Now assume that a government fully reveals who the charlatans are. In this case, there is no longer any possibility of incurring harm, so all consumers would like to consume. Although the equilibrating wage results in little surplus for the consumers who do get to consume, overall consumer surplus is still higher because many more consumers are serviced. Notice that even in this extreme case, introducing a standard does not lead to greater consumer surplus. By introducing a standard into the equilibrium even fewer consumers are serviced, leading to even lower consumer surplus.
Our results imply that if the motivation for regulation was consumer welfare that derives from the possibility of inflicting harm, then we would expect to see certification requirements, not licensing. In fact, almost all of the high-skill professions in the economy feature licensing requirements, not certification requirements. In addition, for certification to increase consumer welfare, the introduction of the regulation should be accompanied by a large increase in market demand for the service. We know of no evidence supporting this implication of our theory.
In this paper, we allow the wage contract to depend on the information available at the time the contract is entered into, but we do not allow it to depend on information revealed after that point. If we did allow for such contracts, then skilled producers could signal their skill through the contract. That is, it is possible that skilled producers could offer a contract that charlatans would choose not to offer, resulting in a fully revealing separating equilibrium. In such an equilibrium there would be no need for regulation. For example, lawyers often take cases on contingency so they only get paid if they win. If the opportunity cost is high enough, such a contract could credibly signal skill perfectly. In cases in which the full information equilibrium is not feasible because it is not possible to use the contract to separate skilled producers from charlatans so the equilibrium features pooling, one can interpret the signal in our model as summarizing all of the information communicated in the contract, and our results would still apply.
article.VII. Conclusion:
Economic models are primarily a means to make a sound argument. The implications of this paper should be viewed in this light. If a government wants to regulate in the interests of consumers, then the implicit assumption made by policy makers—that disclosure and standards help consumers—needs more rigorous justification. Disclosure and standards may indeed be beneficial to consumers but without supporting evidence, policy makers cannot simply assume that this claim is prima facie true.
To justify disclosure and standards, the implication of our model is that policy makers need to make one of the following arguments. They can justify regulating based on Leland (1979), by arguing that the subsequent increase in prices that follows regulation attracts enough new talent to cause a net increase in consumer surplus. Not only would this imply better understanding the wage elasticity in a sector (which is likely to be quite different in the short term and long term), but also showing that the benefit of the increase in quality outweighs the effect of competition identified in this paper.
A second reason why it might make sense to regulate despite the arguments in this paper is if one believes that consumers do not adequately price the cost of facing a charlatan. Here again, evidence should be brought to bear to justify current government policy. As an illustrative example, based on the argument that charlatan money managers take advantage of naive investors, there has been an increasing number of calls for more regulation of the money management industry, with some people suggesting that individuals should be barred from putting their retirement money into active mutual funds. But, in point of fact, Berk and van Binsbergen (2015) find that investors rationally allocate their capital in this industry.
Another reason many people cite for regulation is that charlatans might inflict significant harm. Here again supporting evidence is required to justify regulation. In this case, licensing continues to hurt consumers, yet that is the form the regulation of professions like medicine, which have the potential to inflict significant harm, takes. More to the point, to justify certification regulation, regulators would need to show that large uncertainty exists on the quality of producers and that the opportunity cost to enter the industry is low.
Finally, our analysis does not consider other forms of regulation, such as price controls or transfers between consumers and producers. The possibility exists that by expanding the strategy set beyond certification and licensing, regulators could help consumers. In light of the results in our paper, an analysis of whether other forms of regulation would be beneficial to consumers might be warranted.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
